title: Mastitis in Nursing Cats: Signs, Risks, and Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your nursing cat has hot, hard mammary glands, it may be mastitis. Learn why this condition requires prompt veterinary attention and triage steps.
content_html:
Understanding Mastitis in Nursing Cats

When a nursing mother cat develops hot, hard, or swollen mammary glands, it is often a sign of mastitis. This is a bacterial infection of the milk ducts that can occur during lactation. The condition is painful for the mother and can cause the milk to become toxic or cease production, which puts the health of the kittens at risk.

Is This an Emergency?

The urgency level for mastitis is Medium. While it is rarely an instant life-or-death situation, it requires veterinary care within 12 to 24 hours. If the mother cat develops a high fever, becomes lethargic, or if the glands turn a dark purple or black color, the urgency increases to High, as this may indicate systemic infection or gangrene.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Providing a clear photo of the affected mammary glands to your veterinarian or a triage assistant is incredibly helpful. Visual evidence allows a professional to assess the level of inflammation, look for skin discoloration, and check for any visible abscesses or discharge. This helps determine how quickly the cat needs to be seen and whether it is safe for the kittens to continue nursing from those specific glands.

faqs.question: Should I let the kittens keep nursing if the mother has mastitis?
faqs.answer: In many cases, it is actually beneficial for kittens to continue nursing to help clear the blockage, provided the mother is not in too much pain and the milk is not severely contaminated. However, you must consult your vet first, as systemic infection may require you to hand-rear the kittens temporarily.

faqs.question: What are the common symptoms of feline mastitis?
faqs.answer: Common signs include mammary glands that are firm, hot to the touch, and painful. You may also notice the mother cat being reluctant to nurse, kittens crying due to hunger, or the milk appearing thick, bloody, or discolored.

faqs.question: How is mastitis typically treated by a vet?
faqs.answer: Treatment usually involves a course of feline-safe antibiotics and pain management. In some cases, your vet may recommend warm compresses or cabbage leaf wraps to reduce swelling and encourage milk flow.
